paragraphs.context: used to implement the EDA interface. 6.2.3 SEMI E/32 Role in Security 6.2.3.l SEMI 132- Specification for Equipment Client Authentication and Authorization, defines the mechanism to restrict access to the equipment resource using the concept of "Authentication" and "Authorization." In an EES enabled factory, multiple equipment engineering applications co-exist. An equipment engineering application is represented as a client for the equipment. Not all clients are allowed to access the equipment information, in particular the metadata, or to request data from the equipment. Only client(s) allowed to access specific equipment in the factory are permitted to acquire the information from the equipment. SEMI El 32 en ures this by an authentication and authorization mechanism. 6.2.3.2 EDA communication between a client and equipment is enabled by the ession establishment defined in SEMI El32. All service messages defined in SEMJ El25 and SEMI El34 are exchanged using authenticated sessions as defined in SEMI El32. Clients allowed to access the equipment through the EDA interface may have different levels of access to the equipment-defined functionality. Authorization is the mechanism that ensures which equipment functionality the client is permitted to access. 6.2.3.3 The definition of privileges by using ACL Entries as defined in SEMl El32 restricts client access to a range of equipment features for the authenticated client. The client acce ing the equipment features through the authenticated session must have a privilege(s) assigned to it beforehand through an ACL Entry. paragraphs.context: collected data. 6.2.5.2 The client may require continuous data collection from the equipment. The client may also request temporary information about the equipment's condition or status. SEMl El34 provides two types of data collecting mechanisms for these purposes. One mechanism is based on the data collection plan (DCP) request and the other is an ad-hoc data request. ln DCP requests, the desired data are automatically collected based on the instructions defined by the DCP and reported in data collection repom. A DCP specifies the data set to be collected, the sampling method, the buffering, and persistence. An ad-hoc data request is the mechanism for on-demand data collection. The client obtains the necessary information for a data collection request by querying the metadata from the equipment as defined by SEMI El25 and SEMI El 20. No interference is expected to continuous data collection due to ad-hoc data requests (on demand data collection). Data collection services are defined in SEMJ E 134. 6.3 The Relationship between SEMI EDA Standards 6.3.1 SEMI EDA Standards 6.3.1.1 Figure 3 shows the relationship among SEMl Standards related to the EDA interface. The actors in Figure 4 represent entities in UML that in turn map into software applications. 6.4.2 Security Admin 6.4.2. I SEMI El 32 provides a general mechanism to allow the factory to establish a Central Administration Model. A Central Administration Model can be used to issue client credentials, establish client authorizations, and possibly client credentials on the equipment. SEMI El32 defines the mechanism and interface for authentication with and without credentials and authorization by defining privileges. However. SEMI E 132 does not provide any mechanism to exchange the credentials. SEMI El32 specifies that only the SecurityAdmin utilizes the operations defined in the Security Administration lnterface. The equipment should support onJy one SecurityAdmin account concurrently. The equipment is also required to perform the SecurityAdmin capabilities on its equipment console. These capabilities allow initial credential-setting during equipment installation or troubleshooting. SecurityAdmin is not intended for use under nonnal operational conditions. 6.4.3 Data Consumer or Cliem 6.4.3.1 In SEMI E l 32, the client is defined as an application process that communicates with the equipment to access equipment resources. ln SEMI E134, the data consumer is defined as any software entity that uses the DataCollectionManager interface to manage data collection on the equipment. Thus, the SEMI E 1 34 data consumer is the application that processes or consumes the data sent by the equipment (i.e., the EE application).

However. if the data are defined by the equipment metadata in association with the exception, the data will automatically be included in the report. 8.1.3 Event Request 8.1.3. 1 An event, as defined in the equipment metadata, can be used to collect data by using an event request. The data to be collected when an event occurs can be specified in the event request according to the metadata definition. 8.1.4 Trace Request 8. I .4. I A specific data set can be collected from the equipment using a specified collection period as defined by a trace request. An interval of data collection, the number of data sets to be collected, and the number of data set.<; to be reported can be defined in a trace request.

paragraphs.context: consistently by using a common time synchronization protocol. 1.2 This Standard defines the TS-Clock object for accessing factory application and equipment time value and synchronization status, describes an example of network time synchronization architecture, and specifies which protocol to use for precise time synchronization of clocks in factory environments that use network communication. 1.3 RFC 5905, TPv4 protocol for time synchronization is specified in this Standard because it is applicable to systems communicating by local area networks supporting multicast messaging including but not limited to Ethernet. This protocol enables heterogeneous systems that include clocks of various inherent precision and stability to synchronize. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard applies to factory components that collect, store, distribute, and process time-sensitive data. Factory components include applications used to manufacture devices, maintain equipment, provide process resources, condition the factory, control processes. In particular, it includes the equipment used in manufacturing. OTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices, and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 Factory component services to be used in conjunction with the TS-Clock object are specific to the interfaces and protocols used by those factory components. 3.2 This Standard does not specify the mechanism for synchronization of components within the equipment. 4 Referenced Standards and Documents 4.1 SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines SEMI E39 - Specification

I Objects defined in this Specification comply with the basic requirements of SEMI E39. 7 Implementation Overview 7.1 Traditionally, factory applications have been implemented in a centralized architecture in which the timing constraints are met by careful attention to programming combined with communication technologies having deterministic latency. In recent years an increasing number of systems utilize a more distributed architecture coupled with mainstream network technologies having less stringent timing specifications than the older technologies. Use of network time synchronization protocols allows the different systems within the factory to remain closely synchronized with a high degree of accuracy, and it relieves the factory host from explicitly setting the factory component's internal clock(s). ln this environment the most widely used technique for synchronizing these clocks is RFC 5905, TPv4 {refer to the Referenced

Factory components include software applications, software/hardware systems, and equipment used in manufacturing. SEMI E148-0222 SEMI 2007, 2014, 2022 semr U.adotOIIIWO" Stratun 1 '11meServer Figure 1 Example of Time Synchronization Stratum Layers 7 .3 As shown in Figure I, a typical networked factory may include several layers where factory components can use time servers for time synchronization. These servers create synchronization layers called stratums that define the synchronization hierarchy of factory component clocks. 8 Prerequisites 8.1 Protocol Requirements TPv4 availability. 8.2 Required Standards - SEMI E39 (OSS), SEMI E 134 ifused by the equipment. 8.3 State Models - There are no prerequisites. 8.4 Objects - There are no prerequisites. 8.5 Service Requirements - SEMI E39 (GetAttr, SetAttr), SEMI El34 (GetParameterValueRequest) if used by the equipment. 9 Requirements 9.1 Facto,y Components Requirements 9. paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E151-1211 (Reapproved 0517) GUIDE FOR UNDERSTANDING DATA QUALITY This Standard was technically approved by the Information & Control Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on December 8, 2016. Available at www.semiviews.org and www.semi.org in May 2017; originally published March 2009; previously published December 2011. 1 Purpose l . l The purpose of this Guide is to provide common terminology and definitions that can be used when addressing the quality of semiconductor equipment generated data. It establishes a set of common definitions that help specify, communicate, address, and discuss the quality of data produced by the production equipment. 2 Scope 2.1 This Guide contains the following component: 2.2 Background and description of an approach to understanding data quality and associated terminology. OTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices, and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 The term 'data,' as utilized in this Guide, is limited to data that is or can be collected from production equipment in the semiconductor industry via a SEMI standard communication interface. 3.2 This Guide does not define the protocol of messaging used to transport or communicate data values. That messaging infrastructure is covered in other SEMI Standards. The data quality definitions, attributes, and associated elements contained in this

Data quality aspects are directly associated with data quality attributes which are represented as rectangular boxes. Data quality attributes which impact data quality are defined in the main body of this Guide and are represented as rectangular boxes. Data quality elements are factors that influence data quality attributes. Data quality elements are represented by rectangles and are associated with a data quality attribute. Data quality associations are represented as a line connecting related data quality aspects and related data quality attributes, or data quality attributes and related data quality elements. I Data Quality - The degree to which the inherent characteristics of data fulfill established requirements.23 This Guide includes data quality related definitions, aspects, attributes, and elements that can be applied to the definition, specification, and interpretation of equipment generated data. 7 .2 Data Quality Aspect - A characteristic of data quality that can be depicted by a set of attributes. In this Guide, the data quality aspects of availability, interpretability, and usability are defined. These aspects are defined because data quality specification, definition, or verification is only possible if data is available, interpretable, and usable. Data quality aspects are represented by an oval shape (see 6 and Figure 2). Figure 2 Data Quality Aspects 7.2.1 Availability - Is the first step in the process of understanding data quality. Availability is achieved if the user has the means, authorization, and privilege to get the data. 7 .2.2 Jnterpretability - Provides the means of understanding and whether the data can be evaluated or that further evaluation is not necessary. Figure 5 Data Usability Attributes and Elements 7.5.1.1 Accuracy - Since the true value cannot be determined exactly, the measured or calculated value of highest available accuracy is typically taken to be the true value.4 Figure 6 shows an example of high and low accuracy results represented as a collection of values within an intended target. Accuracy is directly affected by the bias, range, and drift inherent in the system. See 5 for detailed descriptions of these terms. System drift whether it is cyclic, random, or linear can affect the accuracy of the desired process or measurement result. Unstable systems affect the ability to acquire accurate measurements or accurate data reporting. 7.5.1.2 Precision - The quality of repeatability of measurement data, customarily expressed in terms of the standard deviation of the extended set of measurement results from a well-defined (adequately specified) measurement process in a state of statistical control.1 It is the closeness of agreement among indications obtained by replicate measurements on the same or similar objects under specified conditions. This occurs when a device is driven to a same point multiple times but fails to produce the same results over time. paragraphs.context: is an accumulation of multiple sources. In this document three sources a.re identified: Tool latency, Application latency, and Network latency. See 5 for detail description of these terms. Tool latency is affected by all of the equipment internal hardware and software responsible for retrieving the data and reporting it to the application that requested the information. Usually, network latency is negligible unless the message travels over several networks in which case it becomes significant. Application latency is affected by the hardware and software responsible for applying the data received from the tool. paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E152-0619 SPECIFICATION FOR MECHANICAL FEATURES OF EUV POD FOR 150 mm EUVL RETICLES This Standard was technically approved by the Physical Interfaces & Carriers Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on January 25, 2019. Available at www.semiviews.org and www.semi.org in June 2019; originally published July 2009; previously published February 2014. 1 Purpose 1.1 This Standard specifies EUV Pod for the 150 mm Extreme Ultraviolet Lithography (EUVL) reticle, used to ship, transport and store a 6-inch reticle. The EUV Pod consists of an outer pod and a protective inner pod. The EUV Pod is to be used when a conventional reticle carrier does not meet the requirements ofEUVL. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard is intended to set an appropriate level of specification that places minimal limits on innovation while ensuring modularity and their interchangeability at all mechanical interfaces. Many requirements given in this Specification are in the form of maximum or minimum dimensions with very few required surfaces. No material requirements or microcontamination limits are given in this Specification. 2.2 Because of high attenuation feature ofEUV light, a conventional pellicle film cannot be placed in front ofEUVL reticles. The inner pod is to protect reticles from particle contamination. 2.3 The EUV Pod has the following components and subcomponents. The baseplate of inner pod has two possible configurations depending on the intended usage. They are designated Type A and Type B. Detail configuration requirements for each are shown in Table

Its precise location shall be defined by SEMI EI 00 in 6.13. 5.14 EUV Pod Conveyor Rails -The optional surfaces on the EUV pod bottom for transporting the EUV pod as defined in SEMI EI00. 5.15 Baseplate Registration Holes -The holes on the bottom of the baseplate (labeled A, B, C, D, E, F) which may be used for handling and identification are defined by d I, x27, x35, y30, y35, y43, and =20 in Tables I and 2 and Figure 5. 5.16 Baseplate Identification (JD) Exclusion Area -The area reserved on the baseplate for marking or identification is defined by x36, x37, y8, and y9 in Table I and Figure 8. 5.17 Rear Purge Port Areas -The outer pod door shall contain two rear purge areas. The purge areas are defined by x39, y45, and d3 in Table I and Figure 11. 5.18 Front Purge Port Areas -The outer pod door shall contain two front purge port areas. The purge areas are defined by x40, y46, and d4 in Table I and Figure 12. 6 Related Documents 6.1 A design example of EUV Pod and

A1-1 Notes A 1-1. 1 In addition to the primary and secondary baseplate exclusion volumes defined by 5.8.1, any volume between the top of the outer pod door surface (=2) and the bottom of the baseplate (=7) not interfering with the outer pod door KC pins (see Figure 9) may be used by automation to handle the baseplate. A 1-1 .2 The performance of the EUV Pod with the optional front edge grip exclusion volume from 5.2.2 of the Standard has not been fully evaluated. Users should apply this option in consideration of this information. When duplicated (i.e., and the symbols are used to reference more than one section or paragraph, respectively. 1 Purpose 1.1 The purpose of this Specification is to define a standard equipment capability to report recipe step and process related data to the factory system. The subject of this Specification is the activities of a processing location (i.e., process module or process area) that are related to the execution of a recipe. 1.2 The collection of process data during recipe execution is important to today's factories to support various applications that help optimize equipment processes, finished product quality, yield, and overall factory performance. 1.3 This process data reporting capability provides a factory's applications the means of linking sampled time series data (Traces) and summary process data to related context data. Standard events that document recipe execution in the process module or process area guarantee the availability of such context information.

paragraphs.context: overall factory performance. 1.3 This process data reporting capability provides a factory's applications the means of linking sampled time series data (Traces) and summary process data to related context data. Standard events that document recipe execution in the process module or process area guarantee the availability of such context information. The context data may include such items as identification of process job, equipment recipe, and affected substrates. The reporting capability provides a record of start and/or end of processing, with granularity to the recipe step level. 1.4 This Specification defines two ways to report recipe execution. An equipment may implement both techniques. 1.4.1 For equipment with process modules, reporting of the collection events are process module-specific to support the reporting of process module-specific data parameters. Each process module will typically have a different set of context and process data parameter values. A general event, one that applies to all process chambers, would report the same data. independent of which process module the current event report represents. Such general events do not support reporting of different data sets for each process module, as needed to capture the unique data parameters available from each. 1.4.2 For equipment with continuous flow operation, the equipment reports recipe step activities. This can be for individual substrates or a batch of substrates. The same coUection events and data parameters are available to identify all substrate(s) and recipe activity information. This reporting can also be applied to equipment with process modules. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard applies

The recipe is the user-managed, reusable portion of the set of instructions and settings that determine the processing environment seen by the material. Recipes may be subject to change between runs or processing cycles. An equipment recipe consists of one or more recipe components. 5.3.5 host - the intelligent system that communicates with the equipment. 5.3.6 primary recipe -the recipe component that represents the set of recipe components that make up an equipment recipe. Through the primary recipe, the other recipe components can be identified. It is the identifier of the primary recipe that is supplied to the equipment for processing activities. For example, the ReclD provided to a SEMI E40 ProcessJob is the primary recipe's name or identifier. If the recipe consists of only one component, that component is the primary recipe. A recipe is a called a process program in SEMI E30. 5.3. 7 m11/ri-chamber eq11ipmem -equipment that has more than one process module. 5.3.8 process area - any location where substrates are processed. SEMI E30 refers to the recipe as a 'process program'. 7.3.2 This primary recipe can reference multiple subrecipes. The primary recipe and all subrecipes are individual recipe components. Together. these recipe components make up the equipment recipe. 7 .3 .3 Some or all of the recipe components contain the instructions and settings for processing within each process module or process area. paragraphs.context: recipe. 7 .3 .3 Some or all of the recipe components contain the instructions and settings for processing within each process module or process area. The 'module process' is the activity within a process module executed according to an equipment recipe. The module process does not include any pre-process and post-process activities that do not affect the process module directly. For example, calculation activity that occurs after all effects on the process module conclude is not part of the module process. 7.3.4 The beginning and end of module process activity do not necessarily correspond to the beginning and end of execution of a particular recipe component. 7.3.5 Where substrates are included in recipe execution, the processing instructions from the equipment recipes are executed separately on each substrate or substrate group. Activities for different substrates within an equipment typically occur in parallel. One substrate may be processing in Chamber A, while another is processing in Chamber B and a third is in transit back to the carrier. 7.3.6 Tracking of the recipe execution affecting a process module is independent of bow the execution is initiated. Recipe execution may be initiated remotely, such as through a SEMI E40 ProcessJob. It may also be initiated at the user interface. In some cases, recipe execution may be triggered by the equipment itself. For example, maintenance activities might be triggered on a timed basis. 7.3.7 Process execution within a process module normally consists of a variety of activities happening sequentially and/or in parallel. From the standpoint of the object of the process execution (typically a substrate), this can be described as a linear sequence of conditions (or combinations of conditions) encountered. 7.3.7.1 Where a change of conditions is deemed significant to the user, a recipe step boundary should be defined. This provides for reporting of this significant change. 7.3.7.2 Reporting of step-related events can provide important information to the host. The Module Process State Model recognizes recipe steps that begin and end within the scope of module processing and provides transitions to report their start and end. 7.3.7.3 When steps are reported. they must be represented as occurring one at a time. The sequencing of the steps is not restricted. It may be simple or contain loops and branches. 7.3.7.4 It is left to the implementer to define the appropriate recipe steps to be reported (if any). 7.3.7.5 The granularity of the steps defined is an implementation decision and may not represent all changes to recipe execution conditions. 7.3.8 Each module process activity is tracked and reported separately. SEMI E157-1125 SEMI 2010, 2025 semr 1.4 Recipe Parameters 7.4.1 The recipe specification can be modified by the use of recipe parameters. Recipe parameter values may be supplied from several sources. 7.4.2 They may come from the factory host, for example as ProcessJob Variable Parameters. However, the recipe parameter values used by a recipe component or recipe step may not correspond to the Variable Parameter values supplied during job setup. 7.4.2.1 Recipe parameters may be passed from higher-level recipes to subrecipes. For example, a primary recipe may contain a fixed value for the process time, which is passed as a recipe parameter to a recipe component at run time. 7.4.2.2 A recipe parameter may also be supplied by the equipment based on an internal tuning algorithm performed during recipe execution (e.g., wafer-to-wafer control). 7.4.3 The source of the recipe parameters is not the topic of this specification. paragraphs.context: execution on the process module. 7.7 Process Modules 7.7.1 An important aspect of implementing Module Process Tracking is the designation of process modules and the assignment of state model instances. 7. 7 .2 In most cases, the process modules are easily identified. It is typicaJly a location where processing happens to each substrate, in tum, as it progresses through the equipment. The equipment might have one or more process modules. 7.7.3 In a few cases, equipment may have a process module where multiple substrates can be processed independently, in separate threads of recipe execution. To comply with this Specification, the implementer must represent that process module as a parent process module containing multiple child process modules. ln each of these child process modules, only one process thread can execute at a time. In this case, the parent would have no Module Process State Model. but each child would hold an instance of the state model. 7.7.3.1 Thus, a process module is a part of the equipment that is capable of executing recipe instructions or that contains one or more child process modules that have this capability. Instances of the Module Process State Model are defined only for those process modules that execute recipe instructions. 7.7.4 Pre- and Post-Processing 7. 7.4.1 The tracking of pre- and post-processing activities, such as equipment internal scheduler runs, computation of recipe settings before processing (e.g .. to adjust for changes in the module conditions), as well as computation of process results etc. can be of

The Step ID parameter identifies the specific subset of recipe instructions that define the step that is being executed. The StepCount parameter is an index that records the number of steps executed while the process module is in the EXECUTING state. qas.question: How does the StepCount calculate the number of steps?

answers.text: It simply counts each step that is actually executed.

qas.question: What is the purpose of Step!D?

answers.text: to remain constant, even if a new step is added and also allows similar steps in different recipes to share a Step!D.

qas.question: What types of additional data should be available for reporting with Module Process state transition events?

answers.text: Additional data related to recipe (step) execution, such as recipe (step) execution duration. values of endpoint sensors etc.

The GENERAL EXECUTIO state is active when the recipe is executing, but no recipe step is active (i.e., before the first step begins. between steps. and after the last step ends). 11.5.5 STEP ACTll'E 11.5.5.1 In the STEP ACTIVE state, a specific. identified recipe step is executing. This recipe step execution affects the substrate. 11.5.5.2 The implementer decides whether to support recipe steps. This decision should be based on the determination that the definition of recipe steps would result in the reporting of events and related data significant to their customers. 11.5.5.3 When STEP ACTIVE is supported, the implementer determines the granularity of the recipe steps defined. If SEMI E40 is not implemented, recipe steps should include setup, initialization, preconditioning and postconditioning substrate activities. Refer to further discussion in 5.3.12 and 7 .3.7. [El 57.00-RQ-OOl 15-00] Support for the STEP ACTlVE state for substrate process tracking is conditional on the implementation's support for recipe steps for that substrate and the executing recipe. [/RQ] 11.5.5.4 The state model is designed so that recipe step execution cannot overlap. R1-1 Introduction R 1-1. I The following sections provide example scenarios for reporting E 157 collection. Collection events outside the scope of SEMI El 57 are not shown, such as when material arrives at a processing location. Also, S6fl2 messages are shown as synchronous, where another S6F 1 1 is not sent until after the S6f 12. However. this is not intended to be a requirement. It is expected that S6FI I and S6Fl2 are asynchronous where the triggering of one S6fl I is not dependent on the S6f 12 reply of another message. paragraphs.context: Process Tracking State Model. This is useful for continuous flow applications where substrate processing can be staggered in the process area. Processing begins when a substrate enters a process area and ends when the substrate exits the process area. The process area might have multiple substrates in the process area at one time like batch equipment but differ because each substrate is processed independently and will be at a different stage of processing. Each substrate in the process area might be executing the same recipe but it could be different for some equipment. The diagram below while oversimplified shows how this might proceed.
